summary: My dad worked for Vietnam Electricity Company, also known as EVN, which is the only company providing electricity services in Vietnam. Because EVN is so large and needs many workers, they even established their own university, the Electric Power University, to train students for careers in electricity. Growing up, it seemed natural for me to follow in my dad's footsteps by attending EPU and then working for EVN after graduation.

The Electric Power University was created by Vietnam Electricity Company to train students with skills for electricity-related careers. After I graduated high school in 2010, the expected path was for me to attend EPU for four years and then get a job at EVN, especially since my dad's long tenure there gave me preferred admission and financial aid. However, I disliked the education system in Vietnam and felt I wouldn't have the freedom to choose my studies at EPU.

My family and I moved to the United States when I was 18, seeking a brighter future and more freedom in choosing my career. The U.S. was portrayed by my family and the media as a land of opportunity, which motivated me to leave Vietnam. We first lived in Boston briefly before settling in La Crosse, Wisconsin, where I repeated high school to learn English and adapt to American culture.

When I was told I could go to the U.S., my first thought was that I would attend Berklee College of Music in Boston, which was my dream. I have a passion for piano, and I feared that staying in Vietnam would mean giving that up. Although I didn't end up going to Berklee, that dream inspired me to convince my parents to move to America for better opportunities.

Boston was the first place my family and I lived when we arrived in the United States. It was there that I initially dreamed of attending Berklee College of Music. Although our stay in Boston was brief, it marked the beginning of my journey adapting to a new culture and pursuing my goals in America.

After Boston, my family and I settled in La Crosse, Wisconsin, where my aunt sponsored us. She thought it best for me to repeat high school to learn English and American culture, which was frustrating at first because I was older than my classmates and struggled with the language. Despite the challenges, I graduated high school there in 2014 and gained confidence in my English skills.

Currently, I am studying Mathematical Biology at the University of Minnesota Twin Cities with hopes of becoming an orthodontist. This career choice is very different from what I would have expected growing up in Vietnam, where I had no say in my future. Attending this university represents my determination to pursue a better future and follow my own dreams.
